items.title: Scaling beyond one: How Airbnb evolved its data architecture for a multi-product world
items.content_snippet: How Airbnb’s data engineers and analytics engineers built a consistent and flexible data modeling framework to support the expansion into Homes, Experiences, and Services. By : Patrick Lam , Namrata Lamba , Jamie Stober With the May 2025 Summer Release, Airbnb redesigned its app, relaunched Experiences, and debuted Services, pushing us beyond our traditional Homes focus. For the data teams, this meant rapidly evolving a decade-old infrastructure to integrate two brand-new product pillars. Our da

items.title: Apache Hudi™ at Uber: Engineering for Trillion-Record-Scale Data Lake Operations
items.content_snippet:
Apache Hudi™ at Uber: Engineering for Trillion-Record-Scale Data Lake Operations
The Foundation of Uber’s Data Platform
Uber operates one of the most diverse and demanding data ecosystems in the world. Every trip taken, order delivered, ad served, or real-time arrival time recalculated generates an unending stream of data. These data points come from hundreds of microservices, thousands of cities, and millions of riders, each with its own velocity, shape, and business-criticality. At the heart o

items.title: Zero-Growth Stack, Real Gains: How Stack Allocation Can Save 10% CPU in Go
items.content_snippet:
Zero-Growth Stack, Real Gains: How Stack Allocation Can Save 10% CPU in Go
Sr Staff Engineer
Introduction
At Uber, around 65% of our services run with Go®, which accounts for more than 2 million cores. Our current scale means that a 1% efficiency improvement across the board accounts for several million dollars. Today, we’re showcasing how we improved one service’s utilization by 10% and how we plan to replicate it.
The Go runtime employs several mechanisms to keep resource usage low (memory), b

items.title: The 5 Layers Every Cloud Commitment Depends On
items.content_snippet:
The 5 Layers Every Cloud Commitment Depends On
VP of Engineering
Introduction
Committing to a long-term hyperscale cloud commitment is a distributed systems problem with a ten-figure price tag. The act of committing to a cloud platform is the beginning of a massive engineering initiative, one that requires fluency in a set of technical domains that many organizations only discover after the ink is dry.
A cloud commitment at this scale is like a construction project. The team that delivers it is

items.title: How Ansible® Automation Powers the Uber Corporate Network at a Global Scale
items.content_snippet:
How Ansible® Automation Powers the Uber Corporate Network at a Global Scale
Network Engineer
Staff Infrastructure Engineer
Introduction
Enterprise networks must ensure seamless uptime while handling daily operational updates. At Uber, we maintain connectivity for all our offices spread across six continents (Asia, Africa, North America, South America, Europe, and Australia/Oceania), with thousands of engineers accessing it daily. Managing a network of this scale requires orchestrating cloud and

items.title: Hybrid Core Allocation: From Overallocation to Reliable Sharing
items.content_snippet:
Hybrid Core Allocation: From Overallocation to Reliable Sharing
Senior Software Engineer
Staff Software Engineer
Introduction
Some time ago, we introduced cpusets into Odin, Uber’s stateful container orchestration system. This change gives us stricter control over how CPU time is allocated, which helps cut down on throttling, and reduces latency, but comes at the cost of assigning full dedicated CPUs.
For years, our vertical CPU scaler operated under the assumptions that CPU usage could be accur

items.title: How Uber Conquered Database Overload: The Journey from Static Rate-Limiting to Intelligent Load Management
items.content_snippet:
How Uber Conquered Database Overload: The Journey from Static Rate-Limiting to Intelligent Load Management
Introduction
Uber’s thousands of microservices handle traffic for over 170 million monthly active users: riders, Uber Eats users, drivers, and couriers. At the heart of this infrastructure are Docstore and Schemaless, Uber’s in-house distributed databases built on top of MySQL®. These databases span thousands of clusters, store tens of petabytes of operational data, and serve tens of millio

items.title: Validating Bounding Box Annotations
items.content_snippet:
Validating Bounding Box Annotations
Software Engineering Intern
Machine Learning Engineer II
Senior Machine Learning Engineer
Introduction
Despite advancements in open-source models, enterprises often require use-case-specific models to solve their unique business challenges. Training these models necessitates high-quality data, which is frequently collected via human annotations. While effective, these annotations often suffer from human errors, which affect model quality.
Uber AI Solutions pro

items.title: Database Federation: Decentralized and ACL-Compliant Hive™ Databases
items.content_snippet:
Database Federation: Decentralized and ACL-Compliant Hive™ Databases
Introduction
One of Uber’s data warehouses powering the Delivery business outgrew its original design. More than 16,000 Hive datasets and 10 petabytes from multiple business domains lived inside a single, monolithic database—owned and operated by a centralized delivery Data Solutions team. While this one-big-bucket setup once simplified onboarding and discovery, scale and organizational growth turned the same design into a liab

items.title: How Uber Built an Agentic System to Automate Design Specs in Minutes
items.content_snippet:
How Uber Built an Agentic System to Automate Design Specs in Minutes
Introduction
The Uber Base design system serves thousands of engineers, and every component they ship depends on specs that are accurate, complete, and current. When those specs fall behind, engineering builds from assumptions instead of definitions, which causes inconsistencies and tech debt. At that scale, documentation becomes a dedicated workstream that still struggles to keep pace. This blog describes how Uber’s design sys

items.title: Mental Models That Change How You Think | Bill Gurley
items.content_snippet: Bill Gurley spent years on Wall Street, built his career as a partner at Benchmark, worked through Uber’s hypergrowth era, and now serves on the board of the Santa Fe Institute, where he studies complexity and systems thinking. In this episode, Bill shares the mental models he returns to most, including systems thinking, second- and third-order effects, and the importance of understanding both the bedrock of your field and the bleeding edge. He explains what separates great founders, why storyte

items.title: Ask These 2 Questions Before You Start
items.content_snippet: Discipline gets the credit. Meaning does the work. The strongest focus rarely comes from better apps or blockers. It comes from a clear answer to why the work matters in the first place. #DanielPink #Focus #DeepWork #Purpose -- To find out more about Daniel Pink, his books, and view his resources, visit https://danpink.com

items.title: Laugh At YOURSELF
items.content_snippet: Self-deprecation is a tool. Like any tool, use it on the wrong job and it makes things worse. The research comes from Cheng and colleagues at Stanford. The mechanism is simple: laughter reads as confidence when the stakes are low, and as indifference when they're high. #DanielPink #SocialIntelligence #EmotionalIntelligence #Psychology -- To find out more about Daniel Pink, his books, and view his resources, visit https://danpink.com

items.title: Perspective Taking
items.content_snippet: Perspective-taking isn't about agreement but accuracy. The people who need it most, the ones with the most power, are the ones who've practiced it least. Fun Fact: Adam Galinsky ran a 2006 study where he primed participants with power, then asked them to draw the letter E on their forehead. The high-power group drew it readable to themselves. The low-power group drew it readable to others. -- To find out more about Daniel Pink, his books, and view his resources, visit https://danpink.com

items.title: Amazon CloudWatch Application Signals now supports infrastructure, logs, and traces context for faster troubleshooting
items.content_snippet: Amazon CloudWatch Application Signals introduces service health ranking on the application map and new infrastructure, logs, and traces tabs on the service overview page. These capabilities let operators triage unhealthy services and inspect the underlying compute environment, log snippets, and trace details in one place, making it easier to find root causes without switching tools. Customers use Application Signals to monitor the health of distributed applications, but identifying why a service

items.title: Amazon Aurora now supports PostgreSQL major version 18
items.content_snippet: Amazon Aurora PostgreSQL-Compatible Edition now supports PostgreSQL major version 18, starting with version 18.3. This release brings community improvements to query performance and database management, and introduces support for pg_roaringbitmap, a new extension that performs fast, memory-efficient set operations on large collections of integers. This enables use cases such as audience segmentation, tag-based filtering, and permission checks directly in the database without application-layer pr

items.title: AWS Elastic Beanstalk console now integrates CloudWatch Logs in the Logs tab
items.content_snippet: AWS Elastic Beanstalk now provides a CloudWatch Logs integration directly in the environment Logs tab of the Elastic Beanstalk console. Previously, customers had to navigate to the CloudWatch console to find the relevant log groups and log streams for their environments. With this launch, customers can view CloudWatch log events without leaving the Elastic Beanstalk console. The Logs tab displays log groups that an environment streams logs to, as well as log groups matching the aws/elasticbeanst

items.title: Amazon MWAA Serverless now supports Amazon EventBridge notifications
items.content_snippet: Amazon Managed Workflows for Apache Airflow (MWAA) Serverless now supports workflow and task state change events to Amazon EventBridge , enabling data engineering and platform teams to build event-driven automation for their Apache Airflow workflows. Previously, monitoring workflow execution required custom polling logic or manual observation. With this launch, MWAA Serverless can emit events when workflows transition between states, including started, running, succeeded, or failed, and when ind

items.title: Amazon Managed Service for Prometheus now supports Native Histograms
items.content_snippet: Amazon Managed Service for Prometheus now supports ingestion, storage, and querying of Prometheus native histograms, enabling customers to capture high-resolution metric distributions with greater precision and lower cardinality than classic histograms. DevOps engineers, site reliability engineers, and platform teams monitoring latency, request durations, and other distributions can now get more accurate percentile calculations without pre-defining bucket boundaries or managing high-cardinality

items.title: Amazon Managed Service for Prometheus now supports out of order sample ingestion
items.content_snippet: Amazon Managed Service for Prometheus now supports out-of-order sample ingestion and a workspace-level rule query offset. All workspaces have a default out-of-order time window of 1 minute, allowing the workspace to accept metric samples arriving outside strict chronological order. You can adjust this window to match your ingestion patterns or set it to 0 to disable the feature and discard out-of-order samples. You can also configure a global rule query offset that introduces a delay before rule

items.title: How Preply combines AI and human tutors to personalize learning
items.content_snippet: What happens when one of the world's largest language-learning platforms combines human expertise with AI? In this customer story, Preply shares how it's using OpenAI to create more personalized learning experiences for learners around the world—while giving tutors more time to focus on teaching, motivation, and human connection. Hear from Preply's Co-founder & CTO, Dmytro Voloshyn, Staff Product Manager, Emily Stott, and tutor Michelle Garcia Ramos as they discuss how AI is transforming languag

items.title: Debug web apps with browser use in Codex
items.content_snippet: Codex can now use the Chrome DevTools Protocol (CDP) in Browser Use to inspect console logs, runtime errors, local storage, applied styling, network traffic, and performance profiles while working on web applications. In this demo, Codex investigates a slow chat app by profiling interactions and inspecting network requests, then fixes the issues and supports the changes with clear measurements. To use this functionality, enable Developer mode in the Browser settings of your Codex app and approve

items.title: Codex for Creatives: Riff, Design, Ship
items.content_snippet: “Codex is an art director or a creative director for me, helping me riff on ideas,” - Chad Nelson, creative specialist at OpenAI. Don’t sleep on Codex for creatives. It understands brand books, style guides, fonts, composition and can create ship-worthy full funnel campaign visuals in a few hours. Put Codex to work. Tell us what you create. https://lnkd.in/dnxrM282

items.title: Life Near OpenAI's Stargate Project
items.content_snippet: For more than 40 years, Tom and Lisa Perini have welcomed visitors to their ranch and restaurant in Buffalo Gap, Texas, just outside Abilene. As new investment and AI infrastructure arrive in the region, they've seen firsthand how growth can create new opportunities for local businesses while raising important questions about preserving the character of rural communities. In this conversation, the Perinis reflect on what makes their community special, why they believe small towns need opportunit

items.title: Customer Ignite Talk: Antonio Bravo Acin (Global Head of AI Transformation, BBVA) & OpenAI
Build sales account strategies and outreach with Codex
items.content_snippet: See how the sales plugin for Codex helps sales teams move from account context to seller-ready work. In this demo, Codex prioritizes prospect accounts from Salesforce, creates a prospecting plan, drafts account-specific outreach, and sets up a morning cadence to keep the plan updated when account status changes. More than 5 million people now use Codex every week, with sellers, marketers, operators, designers, researchers, investors, and bankers among the fastest-growing users. Learn more: https

items.title: Creating black hole simulations with Codex
items.content_snippet: To learn more about black holes, computational astrophysicist Chi-kwan “CK” Chan is working to create the first moving image of one. CK creates simulations and compares them to observational data gathered at telescopes – but these don’t always match up. CK creates simulations and compares them to observational data gathered at telescopes – but these don’t always match up. So he uses Codex to generate new algorithms to test. It would normally take him ten days to create ten new algorithms. With C

items.title: Training to cycle across Antarctica | with ChatGPT
items.content_snippet: In November, @jamesbensonking will attempt to become the first person to cycle solo and unsupported from the edge of Antarctica to the South Pole, a journey that could take up to 60 days. But for James, the real challenge begins long before he sets foot or wheel on the ice. With no existing blueprint to follow, James uses ChatGPT to build and refine his training in real time, bringing together multiple disciplines, logging ideas, and troubleshooting problems as they arise. Follow James’ journey

items.content_snippet: See how the creative production plugin for Codex helps marketing teams move from product and campaign context to visual concepts, asset variations, and editable creative. In this demo, Codex creates a campaign mood board, refines visual directions, generates a launch asset, and hands it off for final edits. More than 5 million people now use Codex every week, with marketers, creators, operators, designers, researchers, investors, and bankers among the fastest-growing users. Learn more: https://o

items.content_snippet: “Codex is essential in our finance workflows today. It's not just a niche tool for coding,” Wei Loke, Enterprise Platforms and Technology. Wei leads AI initiatives across the finance org and sees our OpenAI teams leveraging Codex to build: ✅ Internal apps across procurement and accounting ✅ Slides and memos ✅ Month-end executive reports ✅ Custom dashboards for vendor-rich reviews ✅ Journal entry preparation support What will you build? Codex is for every role, tool and workflow: https://lnkd.in/

items.title: Why Can't Anyone Answer Questions About the Business? — Garrett Galow, WorkOS
items.content_snippet: Every business question that needs SQL follows the same loop: explain the question, wait for an engineer, get an answer, realize it needs one more join, share a one-off in Slack, repeat. Garrett Galow from WorkOS built Studio to break that loop — an internal workspace where anyone can ask questions against Snowflake, Linear, and Notion in natural language and get answers or reusable widgets without filing a request. The widgets are the interesting part: the LLM writes them once as declarative Ja

items.title: The agent-ready web: Simplify user actions with WebMCP — Tara Agyemang, Google
items.content_snippet: Buying two concert tickets costs an AI agent the entire DOM, the accessibility tree, a screenshot, pixel coordinate math, and then a click that might miss because an ad just loaded and shifted the layout. Tara Agyemang from the Google Chrome team introduces WebMCP, a proposed web standard that replaces that process with structured tools: instead of guessing what your site does, agents get a menu of named, typed, described actions they can call directly. The talk covers two implementation paths.

items.title: How to Keep Shipping When You Walk Away from Your Desk — Zack Proser, WorkOS
items.content_snippet: Simon Willison fires up four parallel agents and is wiped out by 11am. That is the problem Zack Proser is solving: not that the tools are too slow but that human attention is still the hard constraint. His loop: voice brief at 184 words per minute, agent dispatched to an isolated git worktree, laptop closed, progress checked from a phone on LTE miles away via remote control. The talk covers four layers that make this sustainable: signal agents that read Slack and Linear on a loop so you never op

items.title: Stop Making Models Bigger, Make Them Behave — Kobie Crawdord, Snorkel
items.content_snippet: Qwen 3 235B was asked for YouTube's year over year ad revenue growth from 2023 to 2024. It queried a table that didn't exist, tried again, got nothing back both times, and hallucinated an answer. The 4B model Snorkel finetuned with RL called `get_table_name` first, inspected the schema, ran a query, hit a column error, self-corrected, and got the right answer. The training run cost under $500. Kobe Crawford covers why tool discipline matters more than reasoning depth for this class of tasks, how

items.title: Sovereign Escape Velocity: Ownership w Open Models — Gus Martins, & Ian Ballantyne, Google DeepMind
items.content_snippet: Gemma 4's 31B model sits fourth on the LM Arena open model leaderboard. The models around it are at least twice as large; some are 20 times larger. It runs on a single GPU. Competitors at comparable quality need four or five. Ian Ballantyne and Gus Martins walk through what that size efficiency unlocks: running on a Pixel phone (the E2B and E4B models use 2B and 4B of GPU memory despite having more parameters), deploying a medical variant on two GPUs for an entire hospital, and running parallel

items.title: Self Driving Products: Product Signals to Pull Requests — Joshua Snyder, PostHog
items.content_snippet: A rage click, a 2am error spike, a customer Slack message — today each sits until a developer notices, triages, tickets, and writes a fix. PostHog is building a pipeline that collapses that chain: signal arrives, a background agent groups it with related errors and session replays, researches the codebase, and opens a PR. You wake up to green PRs instead of dashboards. Three lessons from building it: off the shelf embedding models cluster signals by structural similarity rather than meaning, so

items.title: GPU Cloud Deployment Without Leaving Your IDE — Audry Hsu, RunPod
items.content_snippet: The iteration cycle before Flash: commit, push, build a Docker image, pull it from the registry, load it onto a server, allocate a GPU, then find out if it works. Audrey Hsu demos what replacing that with a single decorator looks like — add `@flash.endpoint` to an async Python function and it deploys to GPU cloud from your IDE, with hot reload so a model swap is one line of code rather than a container rebuild. The second demo chains three models: Qwen 3 generates image prompts, DreamShaper rend

items.title: RAG is dead, right?? — Kuba Rogut, Turbopuffer
items.content_snippet: Cursor added semantic search and measured a 24% increase in answer accuracy on their composer model, a 2.6% gain in code retention in large codebases, and a 2.2% drop in dissatisfied user requests. Those numbers look small until you factor in that semantic search does not fire on every query. Meanwhile Google search volume for RAG hit a new inflection point in mid 2025 and went through the roof. The Twitter "RAG is dead" discourse and the actual usage curve are moving in opposite directions. Kub

items.title: From Transcription to Live Music: Gemini's Audio Stack — Thor Schaeff, Google DeepMind
items.content_snippet: One API call to Gemini 3 Flash Preview: speaker labels by name, timestamps, emotion tags, language detection with English translation, and a full summary. That is the audio understanding layer that underlies everything else Thor Schaeff demos here, including speech generation directed by a "director's note" rather than picked from a catalogue, and Gemini 3.1 Flash Live, a sound to sound real time multimodal model with thinking baked in rather than cascaded through a separate LLM. The talk ends w
